title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: The Art of Folding: Origami's Unexpected Journey
content.text: Walk into Akira Yoshida's studio and you are immediately surrounded by hundreds of paper sculptures — cranes, dragons, intricate geometric spheres — hanging from every surface. Yoshida has been practising origami for over forty years, and today he teaches the craft to students from around the world. (37) [37] Su camino hacia el origami comenzó en la infancia. [/37] 'My grandmother placed a sheet of paper in my hands when I was five,' he recalls. 'She folded a simple crane in under two minutes. I was completely hooked.' || For centuries, origami was a courtly art in Japan, reserved for ceremonial purposes such as wrapping gifts for nobility. Paper was too expensive for everyday folding. (38) [38] El coste del papel cambió su papel social. [/38] As manufacturing techniques improved in the nineteenth century, paper became affordable, and origami spread into schools and homes across Japan. Eventually, the art travelled westward, arriving in Europe and America during the twentieth century. || Today, origami has moved far beyond the traditional crane. Engineers and scientists have begun borrowing its principles to solve real-world problems. (39) [39] Sus aplicaciones van desde el espacio hasta la medicina. [/39] NASA has used origami-inspired folding patterns to design solar panels that pack tightly for launch and unfold reliably in space. Surgeons have drawn on similar principles to create stents that expand once inside the body. || At the same time, a competitive origami scene has emerged. Folders gather at international conventions to share techniques and display works that can take hundreds of hours to complete. (40) [40] La comunidad compite y colabora a la vez. [/40] 'We share diagrams freely,' says Yoshida. 'The joy is in the folding, not in keeping secrets.' || For beginners, the entry point is deliberately simple. A sheet of A4 paper and ten minutes are all that is needed to fold a recognisable animal. (41) [41] La simplicidad del inicio contrasta con la profundidad del arte. [/41] Yet the mathematics underlying complex models — concepts from topology and computational geometry — can occupy researchers for years. || Yoshida believes origami teaches patience in an age of instant gratification. (42) [42] Él ve el origami como una práctica espiritual tanto como artística. [/42] 'When you fold, you cannot rush,' he says. 'The paper tells you when it is ready.'

paragraphs.A: Yet mastery requires years of disciplined practice and a deep understanding of paper geometry.
paragraphs.B: It was only when paper became widely available that ordinary people could participate.
paragraphs.C: He often says that the discipline of folding a perfect crane is as meditative as any form of yoga.
paragraphs.D: Its applications now extend from aerospace engineering to minimally invasive medicine.
paragraphs.E: Competitors admire each other's work openly, often collaborating between events.
paragraphs.F: His passion for the craft began not in a classroom but at his grandmother's kitchen table.
paragraphs.G: The convention circuit has grown so large that some folders travel across continents to attend.

questions.explanation: F conecta directamente con el recuerdo de la abuela que se narra a continuación.
B explica la transición del origami como arte de élite a práctica popular por el precio del papel.
D introduce las aplicaciones científicas antes de los ejemplos de la NASA y la cirugía.
E describe la dinámica de colaboración y competición que ilustra la cita de Yoshida sobre compartir diagramas.
A contrasta la accesibilidad del inicio con la profundidad matemática que se menciona a continuación.
C amplía la idea de la paciencia y la meditación antes de la cita final sobre no tener prisa.
